question: [File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/Web3Properties.java -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: requestTimeout] Can an unprivileged external user flood the web3 JSON-RPC endpoint with concurrent contract-call requests that each exhaust the full requestTimeout (10 seconds), saturating all server threads and preventing any legitimate transaction from being processed and gossiped to the Hiero network?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/Web3Properties.java -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: requestTimeout] Can an unprivileged user craft a single eth_call payload that triggers a maximally expensive EVM execution path, holding a server thread for the full requestTimeout duration, and by repeating this at scale cause a sustained denial-of-service that blocks all gossip-bound transactions from being forwarded?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/Web3Properties.java -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: requestTimeout] Can an unprivileged user exploit the fact that requestTimeout is checked only at SQL statement boundaries (inside statementInspector) to craft a request whose EVM execution phase runs indefinitely without issuing any SQL, bypassing the timeout entirely and monopolizing a thread that would otherwise process gossip-bound transactions?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/Web3Properties.java -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: requestTimeout] Can an unprivileged user send a crafted request that causes ContractCallContext.isInitialized() to return false inside statementInspector, bypassing the timeout check entirely, allowing the request to run unbounded and starve the thread pool of capacity needed to gossip transactions?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/Web3Properties.java -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: requestTimeout] Can an unprivileged user exploit a race condition between ContractCallContext.get().getStartTime() initialization and the statementInspector timeout check, causing the elapsed time calculation to underflow or reset, effectively granting the request an unbounded execution window and blocking gossip of other transactions?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/Web3Properties.java -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: requestTimeout] Can an unprivileged user send a batch of requests timed to each expire just before the requestTimeout threshold, causing repeated QueryTimeoutException storms that overwhelm the error-handling path and prevent the server from processing and gossiping any valid transactions?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/Web3Properties.java -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: requestTimeout] Can an unprivileged user exploit the minimum requestTimeout of 1 second (enforced by @DurationMin(seconds = 1L)) by sending high-frequency requests that each complete just within 1 second but collectively saturate the database connection pool, preventing transaction-processing queries needed for gossip from executing?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/Web3Properties.java -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: requestTimeout] Can an unprivileged user craft a deeply nested contract call that causes the statementInspector to be invoked thousands of times per request, each check adding overhead that cumulatively delays all other requests and prevents timely gossip of transactions?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/Web3Properties.java -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: requestTimeout] Can an unprivileged user send a request that triggers a long-running database query just under the requestTimeout threshold, causing the statementInspector to allow it through, while the cumulative effect of many such requests exhausts DB connections and prevents gossip-related transaction queries from completing?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/Web3Properties.java -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: requestTimeout] Can an unprivileged user exploit the fact that requestTimeout is read once at bean initialization time (long timeout = web3Properties.getRequestTimeout().toMillis()) and cached in the statementInspector lambda closure, meaning a configuration reload does not take effect, allowing an attacker who previously observed a long timeout window to continue exploiting it to block gossip?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/Web3Properties.java -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: maxPayloadLogSize] Can an unprivileged user send a POST request with a payload exactly at maxPayloadLogSize * 10 bytes (the ContentCachingRequestWrapper buffer limit), causing the wrapper to buffer the maximum allowed data and exhaust heap memory when many such requests arrive concurrently, preventing transaction gossip by crashing or stalling the JVM?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/Web3Properties.java -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: maxPayloadLogSize] Can an unprivileged user send a payload slightly larger than maxPayloadLogSize * 10 bytes to overflow the ContentCachingRequestWrapper buffer, causing it to silently truncate the request body, resulting in a malformed transaction that is never gossiped to the network?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/Web3Properties.java -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: maxPayloadLogSize] Can an unprivileged user send thousands of concurrent requests each with a payload of maxPayloadLogSize * 10 bytes, causing the server to allocate maxPayloadLogSize * 10 bytes per request in ContentCachingRequestWrapper buffers, exhausting heap memory and preventing any transaction from being processed and gossiped?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/Web3Properties.java -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: maxPayloadLogSize] Can an unprivileged user craft a payload that, after whitespace removal in getContent(), expands or contracts in a way that bypasses the maxPayloadLogSize truncation check, causing the logger to write an unbounded string to disk and filling the log volume, which indirectly prevents the node from processing and gossiping transactions?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/Web3Properties.java -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: maxPayloadLogSize] Can an unprivileged user send a payload that triggers the GZIP compression path in getContent() (content.length() > maxPayloadLogSize) but is crafted to be incompressible, causing the GZIPOutputStream to produce output larger than the input, and if the compressed.length() > maxPayloadLogSize check is also true, the raw content is still logged at full size, filling disk and blocking gossip?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/Web3Properties.java -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: maxPayloadLogSize] Can an unprivileged user send a 5xx-triggering request with a payload far exceeding maxPayloadLogSize, exploiting the condition 'content.length() > maxPayloadLogSize && status < 500' being false for 5xx responses, causing the full untruncated payload to be logged and filling disk storage, which prevents the node from writing transaction records needed for gossip?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/Web3Properties.java -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: maxPayloadLogSize] Can an unprivileged user craft a JSON payload where the 'data' field is positioned such that the DATA_PATTERN regex in reorderFields() causes catastrophic backtracking, consuming CPU for an extended period and preventing the server from processing and gossiping legitimate transactions?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/Web3Properties.java -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: maxPayloadLogSize] Can an unprivileged user send a request with a payload containing deeply nested JSON or special Unicode characters that, after StringUtils.deleteWhitespace() processing, produces a string significantly larger than the original, bypassing the maxPayloadLogSize buffer allocation and causing memory exhaustion that prevents transaction gossip?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/Web3Properties.java -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: maxPayloadLogSize] Can an unprivileged user exploit the ByteArrayOutputStream(content.length() / 4) initial allocation in the GZIP path to cause repeated buffer reallocations for incompressible content, generating excessive garbage collection pressure that stalls the JVM and prevents timely gossip of transactions?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/Web3Properties.java -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: requestTimeout] Can an unprivileged user send a request that causes the statementInspector to throw QueryTimeoutException repeatedly in a tight loop (e.g., via a contract that issues many small SQL queries), causing exception-handling overhead to monopolize CPU and prevent other transactions from being gossiped?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/Web3Properties.java -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: requestTimeout] Can an unprivileged user exploit the System.currentTimeMillis() clock source used in the statementInspector timeout check to cause a time-of-check/time-of-use (TOCTOU) race where a request that should have timed out continues executing, blocking a thread needed for transaction gossip?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/Web3Properties.java -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: requestTimeout] Can an unprivileged user send a crafted eth_estimateGas request that triggers the iterative gas estimation algorithm (up to maxGasEstimateRetriesCount=20 iterations), each iteration resetting or extending the effective execution window beyond requestTimeout, preventing the timeout from firing and blocking gossip of other transactions?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/Web3Properties.java -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: requestTimeout] Can an unprivileged user send a request that causes the ContractCallContext to be initialized with a manipulated startTime (e.g., far in the future), causing the elapsed time check (elapsed >= timeout) to never be true, allowing the request to run indefinitely and starve threads needed for transaction gossip?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/Web3Properties.java -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: requestTimeout] Can an unprivileged user send a request that triggers a database deadlock scenario, where the statementInspector timeout check fires but the QueryTimeoutException is swallowed or retried internally, causing the thread to remain blocked beyond requestTimeout and preventing gossip of other transactions?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/Web3Properties.java -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: requestTimeout] Can an unprivileged user exploit the fact that requestTimeout applies per-request but not per-connection, by opening many persistent HTTP connections each sending slow requests, exhausting the server's connection pool and preventing legitimate transaction-gossip requests from being accepted?
